expanded_description:
The Fisher equation is one of the most fundamental relationships in monetary economics. It links nominal interest rates (the rates you see quoted on loans and bonds), real interest rates (the inflation-adjusted cost of borrowing), and expected inflation. Named after economist Irving Fisher (1867-1947), the equation explains how inflation expectations are embedded in interest rates and why lenders demand compensation for the expected erosion of purchasing power.

The Fisher equation is expressed as: $i = r + \pi^e$, where $i$ is the nominal interest rate, $r$ is the real interest rate, and $\pi^e$ is the expected inflation rate. The equation says that the nominal interest rate equals the real interest rate plus expected inflation. If lenders expect 2% inflation, they will add 2% to the real interest rate to ensure they earn the desired real return. Conversely, if expected inflation rises, nominal interest rates rise one-for-one (the Fisher effect), leaving real interest rates unchanged.

Understanding the Fisher equation is essential for interpreting interest rate movements, evaluating the impact of monetary policy, analyzing bond markets, and understanding the relationship between inflation and interest rates. The equation also implies that unexpected inflation redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers (since real interest rates fall below what was expected), while unexpected deflation redistributes from borrowers to lenders.

In this section, we will derive the Fisher equation, distinguish between ex-ante (expected) and ex-post (realized) real interest rates, analyze the Fisher effect (one-for-one adjustment of nominal rates to expected inflation), examine empirical evidence (including the failure of the Fisher effect at the zero lower bound), and explore implications for monetary policy (the Taylor rule, the zero lower bound, and negative interest rates). You will learn to calculate real interest rates from nominal rates and inflation, understand why nominal rates cannot go below zero (the zero lower bound) but real rates can, and analyze how unanticipated inflation affects borrowers and lenders.

core_concept.principle:
The Fisher equation relates nominal interest rates, real interest rates, and expected inflation:

Fisher Equation (Ex-Ante):
$i = r + \pi^e$
where:
• $i$ = nominal interest rate (stated rate on loans and bonds)
• $r$ = real interest rate (inflation-adjusted cost of borrowing)
• $\pi^e$ = expected inflation rate over the life of the loan

Fisher Equation (Ex-Post):
$i = r_{ex-post} + \pi$
where $\pi$ is actual inflation and $r_{ex-post}$ is the realized real interest rate.

Implications:
• If expected inflation rises by 1%, nominal interest rates rise by 1% (the Fisher effect), leaving real interest rates unchanged.
• The real interest rate is the return lenders expect to earn in terms of purchasing power.
• Unexpected inflation benefits borrowers (they repay with cheaper dollars) and harms lenders.
• Unexpected deflation benefits lenders and harms borrowers.

Derivation:
Let $r$ be the real interest rate. A loan of \$1 today grows to $1 + i$ nominal dollars in one year. In real terms, that's $(1+i)/(1+\pi^e)$. The real return is $1 + r = (1+i)/(1+\pi^e)$. For small $i$ and $\pi^e$, this approximates $i \approx r + \pi^e$.

Zero Lower Bound (ZLB):
Nominal interest rates cannot fall below zero (people can hold currency at 0%). Therefore, $i \geq 0$. This implies $r \geq -\pi^e$. Real interest rates can be negative if expected inflation is positive.

Fisher Effect in the Long Run:
In the long run, real interest rates are determined by real factors (productivity, thrift). Changes in expected inflation pass through one-for-one to nominal rates, leaving real rates unchanged.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine you are a lender. You have \$100 today. You can either keep it under your mattress or lend it to a borrower who promises to repay \$105 in one year (5% nominal interest). If you expect prices to rise by 2% over the year (inflation), your \$105 will have purchasing power equivalent to $105/1.02 \approx \$102.94$ in today's dollars. Your real return is about 2.94% (not 5%). The Fisher equation captures this: $i = r + \pi^e$, so $5\% = r + 2\%$, so $r = 3\%$.

Now suppose expected inflation rises to 4%. Lenders will demand higher nominal rates to maintain their real return. Borrowers will be willing to pay higher nominal rates because they expect to repay with cheaper dollars. The Fisher effect predicts that nominal rates will rise to 7% ($i = 3\% + 4\%$), leaving the real rate unchanged at 3%.

If actual inflation turns out to be 6% instead of the expected 4%, the ex-post real rate is $i - \pi = 7\% - 6\% = 1\%$. Lenders earned only 1% real return, not the 3% they expected. Borrowers benefited from unexpectedly high inflation.

The Fisher equation explains why nominal interest rates are high in high-inflation countries (e.g., Argentina, Turkey) and low in low-inflation countries (e.g., Switzerland, Japan). It also explains why real interest rates can be negative: if $i = 2\%$ and $\pi^e = 3\%$, then $r = -1\%$. This occurred in the US in the 1970s and after the 2008 crisis when the Fed kept rates below inflation.

image.alt: Diagram: i = r + π^e; arrows showing components: r (real rate, determined by productivity/thrift) and π^e (expected inflation); nominal rate i at the top.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.1: The Fisher equation: nominal interest rate equals real interest rate plus expected inflation.

sections.introduction:
The Fisher equation is one of the most fundamental relationships in monetary economics. It links nominal interest rates (the rates you see quoted on loans and bonds), real interest rates (the inflation-adjusted cost of borrowing), and expected inflation. Named after economist Irving Fisher (1867-1947), the equation explains how inflation expectations are embedded in interest rates and why lenders demand compensation for the expected erosion of purchasing power.

The Fisher equation is expressed as: $i = r + \pi^e$, where $i$ is the nominal interest rate, $r$ is the real interest rate, and $\pi^e$ is the expected inflation rate. The equation says that the nominal interest rate equals the real interest rate plus expected inflation. If lenders expect 2% inflation, they will add 2% to the real interest rate to ensure they earn the desired real return. Conversely, if expected inflation rises, nominal interest rates rise one-for-one (the Fisher effect), leaving real interest rates unchanged.

Understanding the Fisher equation is essential for interpreting interest rate movements, evaluating the impact of monetary policy, analyzing bond markets, and understanding the relationship between inflation and interest rates. The equation also implies that unexpected inflation redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers, while unexpected deflation redistributes from borrowers to lenders.

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: Irving Fisher (1911) The Purchasing Power of Money; Fisher effect; Modern applications (TIPS bonds, Taylor rule, ZLB).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.6.2: Irving Fisher developed the equation linking nominal rates, real rates, and inflation expectations in the early 20th century.

key_concept.definition:
Fisher Equation: The relationship between nominal interest rates, real interest rates, and expected inflation.

Ex-Ante Fisher Equation (Expected):
$i = r + \pi^e$
where $r$ is the ex-ante real interest rate (expected real return), and $\pi^e$ is the expected inflation rate.

Ex-Post Fisher Equation (Realized):
$i = r_{realized} + \pi$
where $\pi$ is actual inflation and $r_{realized} = i - \pi$ is the realized real return.

Exact Fisher Equation (no approximation):
$1 + i = (1 + r)(1 + \pi^e)$ → $r = \frac{1+i}{1+\pi^e} - 1$
For small values, $i \approx r + \pi^e$ is accurate.

Fisher Effect: The prediction that a 1 percentage point increase in expected inflation leads to a 1 percentage point increase in the nominal interest rate, leaving the real interest rate unchanged. In the long run, this holds because real rates are determined by real factors (productivity, time preference), independent of monetary policy.

Zero Lower Bound (ZLB): Nominal interest rates cannot fall below zero (people can always hold currency at 0% nominal return). Therefore, $i \geq 0$ implies $r \geq -\pi^e$. Real rates can be negative if expected inflation is positive.

Unexpected Inflation: If actual inflation differs from expected inflation, real returns differ from expectations. Borrowers gain from unexpected inflation; lenders lose. This is the primary channel of monetary policy transmission in the short run.
key_concept.context: Irving Fisher first articulated the relationship in his 1911 book The Purchasing Power of Money. The Fisher effect was empirically tested in the 1970s-1980s when high inflation led to high nominal rates. During the Great Inflation (1965-1982), nominal rates rose roughly one-for-one with inflation, supporting the Fisher effect. However, at the zero lower bound (2008-2015, 2020-2022), nominal rates could not fall below zero even as inflation fell, breaking the Fisher effect in the short run. The Fisher equation remains a cornerstone of monetary economics, embedded in the Taylor rule (which adjusts nominal rates based on inflation deviations) and in the pricing of inflation-indexed bonds (TIPS). The equation also explains why central banks cannot set real interest rates directly—they can only set nominal rates, and real rates depend on inflation expectations.

image.alt: Graph: Nominal interest rate (i) on y-axis, expected inflation (π^e) on x-axis; 45-degree line showing Fisher effect (one-for-one pass-through); intercept = real interest rate r.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.3: The Fisher effect: a one-percentage-point increase in expected inflation raises nominal rates by one percentage point, leaving real rates unchanged.

classifications.type: Ex-Ante vs. Ex-Post Real Interest Rates
classifications.value: Ex-ante real rate is expected (forward-looking); ex-post real rate is realized (backward-looking)
characteristics: Ex-ante real rate: $r_{ex-ante} = i - \pi^e$ (expected real return, known at time of lending)
Ex-post real rate: $r_{ex-post} = i - \pi$ (actual real return, known only after inflation realized)
Difference between ex-post and ex-ante = $\pi^e - \pi$ (unexpected inflation)
If $\pi > \pi^e$, borrowers gain (repay with cheaper dollars); lenders lose
If $\pi < \pi^e$, borrowers lose; lenders gain
TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities) provide a direct measure of ex-ante real rates
classifications.behavior:
The distinction between ex-ante and ex-post real rates is crucial for understanding who benefits from inflation surprises. When a loan is made, the lender and borrower agree on a nominal rate based on their expectations of future inflation. The ex-ante real rate is what they expect to earn/pay. After the fact, actual inflation may differ. If inflation is higher than expected, the ex-post real rate is lower than expected. Borrowers benefit (they repay in cheaper dollars), and lenders are harmed.

This mechanism is central to monetary policy transmission. When the central bank unexpectedly increases inflation (through expansionary policy), it reduces the real burden of debt, stimulating spending by borrowers. This is one reason why monetary policy can affect real economic activity in the short run.

In financial markets, TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities) provide a direct measure of ex-ante real rates. The yield on a TIPS bond is the real interest rate because the principal is adjusted for inflation. The difference between the nominal Treasury yield and the TIPS yield (the breakeven inflation rate) is the market's expectation of future inflation ($\pi^e$).

image.alt: Timeline: Loan made at time t (i fixed, π^e); time t+1 (π realized); ex-ante r = i-π^e; ex-post r = i-π; unexpected inflation = π-π^e.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.4: Ex-ante vs. ex-post real interest rates: the difference is unexpected inflation.

examples.process: Calculating Ex-Ante and Ex-Post Real Rates
examples.explanation: Nominal rate = 5%, expected inflation = 2%, actual inflation = 4%. Ex-ante real rate = 5% - 2% = 3%. Ex-post real rate = 5% - 4% = 1%. Borrowers gain 2% real return (paid 1% real instead of 3% expected). Lenders lose 2% real return.

10-year nominal Treasury yield = 4.5%. 10-year TIPS yield = 2.0%. Breakeven inflation = 4.5% - 2.0% = 2.5%. Markets expect 2.5% average inflation over the next 10 years. If actual inflation turns out to be 3.0%, TIPS holders earn 2.0% real (guaranteed), nominal bond holders earn 4.5% - 3.0% = 1.5% real (worse than TIPS).

classifications.value: In the long run, nominal interest rates adjust one-for-one with expected inflation, leaving real rates unchanged
characteristics: Fisher effect: $\Delta i = \Delta \pi^e$, holding $r$ constant
Real interest rate determined by real factors (productivity, time preference, demographics)
Empirically holds in long-run cross-country data and in high-inflation periods
Fails at the zero lower bound (nominal rates cannot go negative)
Fails in short run due to sticky expectations or monetary policy lags
classifications.behavior:
The Fisher effect is a long-run proposition. If expected inflation rises permanently from 2% to 4%, lenders will demand higher nominal rates to protect their real returns. Borrowers will be willing to pay higher nominal rates because they expect to repay with cheaper dollars. In equilibrium, nominal rates rise by 2 percentage points, and real rates return to their original level.

Evidence for the Fisher effect comes from cross-country comparisons: countries with high inflation (Argentina, Turkey) have high nominal interest rates (often 30-50%); countries with low inflation (Switzerland, Japan) have low nominal rates (0-2%). Time-series evidence from the US also supports the Fisher effect in the long run: during the Great Inflation (1965-1982), inflation rose from 1% to 9%, and nominal rates rose from 4% to 14%—roughly one-for-one.

However, the Fisher effect fails in the short run and at the zero lower bound. After the 2008 crisis, inflation fell to 1%, but nominal rates could not fall below zero (they hit the ZLB at 0.25%). Real rates rose (because inflation fell faster than nominal rates), deepening the recession. This is why the Fed engaged in QE and forward guidance—to lower real rates when nominal rates were stuck at zero.

image.alt: Scatter plot: Inflation (x-axis) vs. nominal interest rates (y-axis) for 50+ countries; strong positive correlation; US point in lower left; Argentina in upper right; slope near 1.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.5: Cross-country evidence for the Fisher effect: high-inflation countries have high nominal interest rates; the slope is close to 1 in the long run.

examples.process: Fisher Effect During the Great Inflation (US)
examples.explanation: 1965: Inflation = 1.5%, 3-month Treasury bill rate = 4.0%, real rate = 2.5%. 1980: Inflation = 12.5%, T-bill rate = 15.0%, real rate = 2.5%. Nominal rates rose by 11 percentage points; inflation rose by 11 percentage points; real rate remained around 2.5%. The Fisher effect held in the long run.

examples.process: Failure of Fisher Effect at the ZLB (Japan)
examples.explanation: Japan's inflation has averaged near 0% for decades, but nominal rates have also been near 0% (not negative). According to the Fisher effect, if inflation is 0%, nominal rates should equal real rates (say 2%). But nominal rates cannot go negative, so if real rates are negative (as in a liquidity trap), nominal rates cannot adjust. This is a failure of the Fisher effect at the zero lower bound.

classifications.type: Zero Lower Bound (ZLB) and Negative Real Rates
classifications.value: Nominal interest rates cannot fall below zero, but real rates can be negative if expected inflation is positive
characteristics: ZLB: $i \geq 0$ (currency pays 0%, so no one would lend at negative nominal rate)
Real rate can be negative: $r = i - \pi^e \leq -\pi^e$
If $\pi^e = 2\%$ and $i = 0\%$, then $r = -2\%$ (negative real rate)
Negative real rates stimulate borrowing and spending (inflation tax)
Central banks use QE and forward guidance to lower real rates when nominal rates are at ZLB
Negative nominal rates possible in practice (ECB, BOJ, Swiss National Bank) with frictions (storage costs for currency)
classifications.behavior:
The zero lower bound (ZLB) is the constraint that nominal interest rates cannot fall below zero because people can always hold currency (which pays 0% nominal return) rather than lending at negative rates. This constraint becomes binding when the central bank would like to set negative nominal rates to stimulate the economy (as in a deep recession).

Even with nominal rates at zero, real rates can be negative if expected inflation is positive. For example, if the central bank commits to 2% inflation and sets $i=0\%$, then $r = -2\%$. Negative real rates encourage borrowing and discourage saving, stimulating aggregate demand. This is why central banks use forward guidance (promising future inflation) to lower real rates when they cannot lower nominal rates further.

Some central banks (ECB, BOJ, Swiss National Bank) have pushed nominal rates slightly negative (e.g., -0.5%) by imposing costs on reserves. However, there is a practical limit: if rates become too negative, people will hoard physical currency. Estimates suggest the lower bound is around -1% to -2% (depending on storage and insurance costs).

image.alt: Graph: Time series of nominal rate (0% at ZLB) and real rate (negative when inflation > 0); shaded area shows negative real rates at ZLB.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.6: At the zero lower bound, nominal rates are stuck at zero, but real rates can be negative if inflation is positive.

examples.process: Negative Real Rates in the US (2009-2015)
examples.explanation: Fed funds rate = 0.25%, core PCE inflation = 1.5%, real rate = -1.25%. The Fed kept nominal rates near zero while inflation was positive, creating negative real rates. This stimulated borrowing and helped the recovery from the 2008 crisis, despite the ZLB constraint.

ECB deposit facility rate = -0.5%. Banks pay 0.5% to park excess reserves at the ECB. This encourages banks to lend rather than hold reserves. However, retail deposit rates cannot go negative (banks would lose customers), so the pass-through is limited. The BOJ also has negative rates (-0.1%).

classifications.value: When actual inflation differs from expected inflation, wealth is redistributed between borrowers and lenders
characteristics: Unexpected inflation ($\pi - \pi^e > 0$) benefits borrowers, harms lenders
Unexpected deflation ($\pi - \pi^e < 0$) benefits lenders, harms borrowers
Magnitude depends on amount of nominal debt outstanding
Government is a large borrower (debt held by public) → unexpected inflation reduces real debt burden
Fixed-income households (pensioners) are lenders → unexpected inflation harms them
Mortgage holders (borrowers) benefit from unexpected inflation
classifications.behavior:
The Fisher equation implies that the ex-post real interest rate ($i - \pi$) equals the ex-ante real rate ($i - \pi^e$) minus unexpected inflation: $r_{ex-post} = r_{ex-ante} - (\pi - \pi^e)$. When inflation is higher than expected, the real return to lenders is lower than expected, and the real cost to borrowers is lower than expected. Wealth is transferred from lenders to borrowers.

This redistribution channel is an important transmission mechanism for monetary policy. When the central bank unexpectedly increases inflation (e.g., by cutting rates), it reduces the real burden of debt. Borrowers (households with mortgages, firms with loans) have more disposable income and spend more. Lenders (savers, pension funds) have less real income and spend less, but borrowers typically have a higher marginal propensity to spend, so aggregate demand increases.

Large unexpected inflation can cause massive redistribution. In the 1970s, unexpected inflation reduced the real value of government debt (benefiting taxpayers) and harmed fixed-income retirees. In the 2021-2022 inflation surge, mortgage holders with fixed-rate mortgages benefited (their real payments fell), while savers lost purchasing power.

image.alt: Diagram: Borrowers (mortgage holders, firms, government) gain from unexpected inflation; Lenders (savers, pension funds, bondholders) lose; arrows show wealth transfer.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.7: Unexpected inflation redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers; the magnitude depends on the amount of nominal debt.

examples.explanation: In the 1970s, many homeowners had fixed-rate mortgages at 5-6%. Inflation rose to 10% unexpectedly. The real interest rate on mortgages became negative (e.g., 6% - 10% = -4%). Homeowners effectively paid back less in real terms than they borrowed. Lenders (savings and loans, bondholders) lost real wealth. This redistribution contributed to the housing boom of the 1970s.

US federal debt held by the public = \$24 trillion. Unexpected inflation of 6% (above the 2% expected) reduced the real value of this debt by approximately \$1.4 trillion (6% of \$24T) in one year—a massive transfer from bondholders (including foreign governments, pension funds, the Fed) to taxpayers. This is one reason why governments may tolerate moderate unexpected inflation.

principles.principle: Nominal Interest Rates Cannot Be Negative (Zero Lower Bound), But Real Interest Rates Can
principles.statement: The zero lower bound (ZLB) on nominal interest rates arises because people can always hold currency, which pays 0% nominal return. No rational lender would lend at a negative nominal rate when they could simply hold cash. However, real interest rates can be negative if expected inflation is positive. When the central bank sets nominal rates at zero and expected inflation is 2%, the real rate is -2%. This negative real rate stimulates borrowing and spending, providing monetary stimulus even when nominal rates are at the ZLB. Central banks can also push nominal rates slightly negative (e.g., -0.5%) by imposing costs on reserves, but there is a practical limit around -1% to -2% due to currency storage costs. Understanding the ZLB is crucial for analyzing monetary policy in deep recessions (2008-2015, 2020) and for evaluating unconventional tools like QE and forward guidance.
principles.application: When the economy is in a liquidity trap (nominal rates at ZLB), the central bank cannot lower nominal rates further. It must instead lower real rates by raising expected inflation (through forward guidance) or by using quantitative easing to compress term premiums. The Taylor rule (which suggests raising nominal rates when inflation rises) must be modified at the ZLB to account for the constraint. The Fed's average inflation targeting (AIT) framework, adopted in 2020, allows inflation to run above 2% for some time to compensate for past misses, which helps lower real rates at the ZLB.
principles.example: From 2009-2015, the Fed kept the federal funds rate at 0-0.25% while inflation averaged about 1.5%. The real interest rate was approximately -1.25% (0.25% - 1.5%). This negative real rate provided substantial stimulus, contributing to the recovery from the Great Recession. The Fed could not have achieved this without the Fisher equation: they set nominal rates at zero and relied on positive inflation to create negative real rates.

image.alt: Graph: Time series of nominal and real interest rates; nominal rate hits zero bound (2009-2015); real rate negative during same period (inflation positive).
image.caption: Figure 7.6.8: At the zero lower bound, nominal rates are stuck, but real rates can be negative, providing stimulus through the Fisher equation.

principles.principle: The Fisher Effect Implies That Monetary Policy Cannot Control Real Interest Rates in the Long Run
principles.statement: In the long run, real interest rates are determined by real factors: productivity growth, time preference, demographics, and global capital flows. Monetary policy can only influence nominal rates and inflation. If the central bank tries to keep nominal rates too low relative to expected inflation, it will create negative real rates, which stimulate borrowing and eventually raise inflation and inflation expectations, causing nominal rates to rise (Fisher effect). Conversely, if the central bank tries to keep nominal rates too high, it will create positive real rates, dampening demand and lowering inflation, causing nominal rates to fall. In equilibrium, the real rate returns to its natural rate (r*), determined by real factors. This is why central banks focus on inflation targeting rather than trying to peg real rates. The natural rate of interest (r*) is unobservable but can be estimated from economic models.
principles.application: Central banks use estimates of the natural rate of interest (r*) to guide policy. The Taylor rule recommends setting the nominal policy rate as $i = r* + \pi^e + 0.5(\pi - \pi^*) + 0.5(Y - Y*)$. If r* falls (due to aging population, low productivity growth), the central bank must lower nominal rates to keep policy neutral. The Fisher effect implies that a change in r* leads to a one-for-one change in nominal rates in the long run, assuming inflation expectations anchored. Failure to recognize a decline in r* can lead to policy being too tight (as in Japan in the 1990s).
principles.example: From 1990 to 2020, the natural rate of interest (r*) in the US fell from about 3% to about 0.5% (due to aging, globalization, low productivity). The Fisher effect implies that if inflation expectations are anchored at 2%, nominal rates should fall from 5% to 2.5%. Indeed, the fed funds rate averaged 5% in the 1990s and 1% in the 2010s. The Fed's policy followed the decline in r*, demonstrating the Fisher effect in action.

image.alt: Graph: Estimated natural rate of interest (r*) 1990-2024; declining from 3% to 0.5%; nominal fed funds rate tracking r* + 2% inflation.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.9: The natural rate of interest (r*) has declined over time; the Fisher effect implies nominal rates should follow r* + expected inflation.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Real Interest Rates and Expected Inflation from Nominal Rates

image.alt: Table: Scenario 1 (i=5%, π^e=2%, r=3%); Scenario 2 (i=8%, π^e=5%, r=3%); Scenario 3 (i=2%, π^e=3%, r=-1%).
image.caption: Figure 7.6.10: Worked example: applying the Fisher equation to calculate real rates and expected inflation.

worked_examples.problem:
Given the following data:

a) Nominal interest rate = 6%, expected inflation = 2%. Calculate the ex-ante real interest rate.
b) Nominal interest rate = 4%, ex-ante real interest rate = 1%. Calculate expected inflation.
c) Nominal interest rate = 3%, actual inflation = 5%. Calculate the ex-post real interest rate. Was inflation higher or lower than expected? Who gained?
d) A 10-year TIPS bond yields 2.0%. A 10-year nominal Treasury bond yields 4.5%. What is the market's expectation of average inflation over the next 10 years?
e) If the Fed wants a real interest rate of 0.5% and expected inflation is 2.5%, what nominal rate should it set?

step_by_step.step: Calculate ex-post real rate and analyze
step_by_step.explanation:
$r_{ex-post} = i - \pi = 3\% - 5\% = -2\%$

Actual inflation (5%) exceeded the expected inflation (unknown). To determine expected inflation, we need additional information. But we can say: if lenders expected 2% inflation, then unexpected inflation = 3%, borrowers gained 3% real wealth. If lenders expected 4% inflation, then unexpected inflation = 1%, borrowers gained 1%. Without knowing $\pi^e$, we cannot say whether inflation was higher or lower than expected—only that actual inflation was 5%.

Breakeven inflation = Nominal yield - TIPS yield = 4.5% - 2.0% = 2.5%

The market expects average inflation of 2.5% over the next 10 years. This is the inflation rate that would make nominal and TIPS bonds have the same real return. If actual inflation is higher than 2.5%, TIPS outperform nominal bonds; if lower, nominal bonds outperform.

$i = r + \pi^e = 0.5\% + 2.5\% = 3.0\%$

The Fed should set the nominal policy rate at 3.0% to achieve a 0.5% real rate given 2.5% expected inflation.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) r = 4%. b) π^e = 3%. c) r_ex-post = -2%. d) Breakeven inflation = 2.5%. e) i = 3.0%.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Fisher equation to calculate real rates, expected inflation, and TIPS breakeven rates.

worked_examples.title: Unexpected Inflation and Wealth Redistribution

image.alt: Table: Loan amount $10,000, i=5%, π^e=2%, π=4%; calculations of ex-ante and ex-post real payments and wealth transfer.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.11: Worked example: calculating the wealth transfer from lenders to borrowers due to unexpected inflation.

worked_examples.problem:
A borrower takes out a 1-year loan of \$100,000 at a nominal interest rate of 6%. The lender expects inflation of 2% over the year. Actual inflation turns out to be 5%.

a) Calculate the expected real interest rate.
b) Calculate the actual (ex-post) real interest rate.
c) Calculate the real value of the repayment in today's dollars (using actual inflation).
d) How much wealth is transferred from the lender to the borrower due to unexpected inflation?
e) If the loan were indexed to inflation (like TIPS), how would the outcome differ?

step_by_step.explanation:
$r_{ex-ante} = i - \pi^e = 6\% - 2\% = 4\%$

The lender expects to earn 4% real return. The borrower expects to pay 4% real cost.

$r_{ex-post} = i - \pi = 6\% - 5\% = 1\%$

The lender actually earned only 1% real return. The borrower actually paid only 1% real cost. Unexpected inflation (3%) reduced the real interest rate by 3 percentage points.

Nominal repayment = $100,000 \times (1 + 0.06) = \$106,000$

Real value of repayment in today's dollars = $106,000 / (1 + 0.05) = $106,000 / 1.05 = \$100,952$

The borrower repays the equivalent of \$100,952 in today's purchasing power, only \$952 more than the original loan amount (a 0.95% real return, close to the 1% real rate).

If inflation had been as expected (2%), the real repayment would have been: $106,000 / 1.02 = \$103,922$

Actual real repayment = \$100,952

Difference (loss to lender, gain to borrower) = $103,922 - 100,952 = \$2,970$

Unexpected inflation of 3% transferred \$2,970 (about 3% of the loan principal) from the lender to the borrower. This is the inflation tax on lenders.

If the loan were indexed to inflation (like a TIPS bond), the nominal repayment would adjust with actual inflation. The contract might specify: repayment = principal × (1 + i_real) × (1 + π).

If the real rate is 4%, then nominal repayment = $100,000 × 1.04 × 1.05 = \$109,200$

Real value of repayment = $109,200 / 1.05 = $100,000 × 1.04 = \$104,000$ (4% real return, as expected).

With indexing, the lender is protected from unexpected inflation. The borrower pays a higher nominal amount but the real cost is unchanged. No wealth transfer occurs. This is why TIPS are attractive to inflation-wary investors.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 4%. b) 1%. c) \$100,952. d) \$2,970 transferred from lender to borrower. e) With indexing, nominal repayment adjusts, real repayment remains \$104,000 (4% real), no wealth transfer.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Fisher equation to quantify the wealth transfer from unexpected inflation and the role of inflation-indexed contracts.

process_flow.process: Lenders and borrowers form inflation expectations
process_flow.description: Economic agents use past inflation, central bank announcements, and economic models to forecast future inflation over the loan horizon.
process_flow.mechanism: Expected inflation ($\pi^e$) is unobservable but can be inferred from surveys (Michigan Survey, SPF), TIPS breakevens, or economic models. Expectations are rational (use all available information) but can be adaptive (based on past inflation) in some models.

image.alt: Diagram: Past inflation + central bank policy + economic conditions → inflation expectations (π^e).
image.caption: Figure 7.6.12: Step 1 - Inflation expectations are formed based on past data, policy announcements, and economic conditions.

process_flow.process: Real interest rate determined by real factors (r*)
process_flow.description: The equilibrium real interest rate (natural rate) is determined by productivity growth, time preference, demographics, and global capital flows, not by monetary policy.
process_flow.mechanism: In the long run, $r = r^*$ (natural rate). In the short run, monetary policy can push real rates away from $r^*$, but with consequences for inflation. The natural rate is estimated using models (Laubach-Williams, Holston-Laubach-Williams) but is unobservable.

image.alt: Diagram: Productivity growth + population aging + time preference + global capital flows → natural real rate (r*).
image.caption: Figure 7.6.13: Step 2 - The real interest rate is ultimately determined by real factors, not monetary policy.

process_flow.process: Nominal interest rate set as i = r + π^e (Fisher equation)
process_flow.description: Lenders demand, and borrowers offer, nominal rates that reflect the expected real return plus expected inflation. The market clears at this nominal rate.
process_flow.mechanism: In competitive markets, the nominal rate adjusts so that $i = r + \pi^e$. If $i$ were higher, borrowers would decrease demand; if lower, lenders would decrease supply. The Fisher equation holds as an equilibrium condition.

image.alt: Diagram: r* + π^e → i (nominal interest rate) through market equilibrium; arrow shows Fisher equation.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.14: Step 3 - The nominal interest rate is the sum of the real rate and expected inflation (Fisher equation).

process_flow.process: Actual inflation realized; ex-post real rate differs if expectations wrong
process_flow.description: After the loan period, actual inflation ($\pi$) is observed. The ex-post real rate ($i - \pi$) may differ from the ex-ante real rate if $\pi \neq \pi^e$.
process_flow.mechanism: Unexpected inflation ($\pi - \pi^e > 0$) benefits borrowers (real cost lower than expected) and harms lenders (real return lower than expected). This redistribution channel is a key transmission mechanism for monetary policy.

image.alt: Diagram: π realized → ex-post real rate = i - π; compare to ex-ante r = i - π^e; difference = π^e - π (unexpected inflation).
image.caption: Figure 7.6.15: Step 4 - Actual inflation determines the ex-post real rate; unexpected inflation redistributes wealth.

process_flow.process: Central bank uses Fisher equation to set policy (Taylor rule)
process_flow.description: The central bank sets the nominal policy rate based on the natural rate, inflation target, and inflation/output gaps, recognizing the Fisher equation.
process_flow.mechanism: Taylor rule: $i = r^* + \pi^e + 0.5(\pi - \pi^*) + 0.5(Y - Y^*)$. The Fed adjusts nominal rates to achieve its inflation and employment goals, knowing that real rates affect aggregate demand. At the ZLB, the Fed uses forward guidance to raise $\pi^e$ and lower real rates when $i$ cannot go negative.

image.alt: Diagram: r* + π^* + policy responses to gaps → target i; at ZLB, forward guidance raises π^e to lower real rates.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.16: Step 5 - Central banks use the Fisher equation in policy rules like the Taylor rule to set nominal interest rates.

image.alt: Master framework: Real factors (productivity, demographics) → r*; Expectations (past inflation, policy) → π^e; Fisher equation combines them into i (nominal rate); actual inflation determines ex-post real rate and redistribution; central bank uses Taylor rule to set i, with ZLB constraint.
image.caption: Figure 7.6.17: Complete framework for the Fisher equation: from real factors and expectations to nominal rates, redistribution, and monetary policy.

summary: The Fisher equation ($i = r + \pi^e$) links nominal interest rates, real interest rates, and expected inflation. The ex-ante real rate ($r = i - \pi^e$) is the expected real return on lending; the ex-post real rate ($i - \pi$) is the realized return after inflation. Unexpected inflation ($\pi - \pi^e > 0$) redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers. The Fisher effect predicts that, in the long run, nominal rates adjust one-for-one with expected inflation, leaving real rates unchanged—a prediction supported by cross-country and long-run time-series evidence. At the zero lower bound (ZLB), nominal rates cannot fall below zero, but real rates can be negative if expected inflation is positive, providing monetary stimulus. Central banks use the Fisher equation in policy rules (Taylor rule) and in the design of unconventional tools (forward guidance to raise $\pi^e$ when $i$ is at ZLB). TIPS bonds provide a direct measure of market-based inflation expectations via breakeven rates. The natural rate of interest ($r^*$)—determined by productivity, demographics, and global capital flows—is the real rate consistent with full employment and stable inflation. Understanding the Fisher equation is essential for interpreting interest rate movements, analyzing monetary policy, evaluating bond markets, and understanding the redistributive effects of inflation.
